food_subgroup: Fried fish and chicken with steamed rice

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for the combined crispy fish and chicken with rice dish; closest_parent 'fried fish food product' used as the primary anchor. The chicken and rice components are not captured by this term.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Fish, fried) used as closest_parent anchor. The composite dish also includes fried chicken pieces and steamed rice, which are not represented by this single code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for this composite Thai fried fish and chicken rice dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate comprising approximately 100 g crispy fried fish fillet, 120 g fried chicken drumettes and wings, and 230 g steamed white rice, totalling approximately 450 g.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated composite from USDA FoodData Central: Fried fish fillet (SR Legacy 15264), Fried chicken drumette (SR Legacy 05062), Cooked white rice (SR Legacy 20045)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct HPB Singapore or FAO/INFOODS SEA entry found for this specific composite Thai restaurant dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-average composition of three USDA SR Legacy components: fried fish fillet (~22% of plate weight), fried chicken drumette/wing (~27% of plate weight), and cooked white rice (~51% of plate weight). Weights are based on the stated 450 g serving description. Per-100 g values are derived from this weighted composite. Confidence is medium due to estimation from components rather than direct dish analysis.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — white rice, steamed (GI 64); composite dish GL estimated from carbohydrate content per serving
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this composite Thai crispy fish and chicken rice dish. The GI of 64 is assigned based on the dominant carbohydrate source being steamed white rice, for which the University of Sydney GI Database reports a GI of 64 (medium). The fried fish and chicken components contribute negligible glycemic carbohydrates. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (64 × 96.75) / 100 ≈ 62.1, where available carbohydrate per serving = 21.5 g/100 g × 450 g = 96.75 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 280 mg per 100 g; a full 450 g serving delivers approximately 1260 mg sodium, representing over 50% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to half a plate and request reduced seasoning or sauce to keep sodium intake within daily targets.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load per serving (approximately 62) driven by a large portion of steamed white rice (GI 64, medium), which can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should reduce the rice portion to approximately 100–120 g cooked and increase the proportion of protein from fish and chicken to moderate the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying of both fish and chicken increases total fat and saturated fat content; cholesterol approximately 52 mg per 100 g; sodium load is also a cardiovascular risk factor.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to occasional consumption, choose grilled or steamed alternatives where available, and avoid additional dipping sauces to reduce saturated fat and sodium intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Crispy Fish and Chicken Rice is a Thai-style combination rice plate dish consisting of deep-fried crispy golden fish fillets and fried chicken drumettes and wings served alongside a generous portion of fluffy steamed white rice. The dish is a popular Thai restaurant offering in Singapore, notably served at Saap Saap Thai. The fish fillets are coated in a light seasoned batter and deep-fried to achieve a crunchy golden exterior while remaining moist inside. The chicken pieces — drumettes and wings — are similarly fried to a juicy, crispy finish. The steamed jasmine rice serves as the starchy base and dominant carbohydrate source. Nutritionally, the dish provides a moderate protein contribution from both fish and chicken, with the rice contributing the majority of carbohydrates and energy. The deep-frying process elevates total fat and saturated fat content compared to grilled or steamed preparations. Sodium content is moderate to high depending on seasoning and sauces. The glycemic index is driven by the steamed white rice component (GI approximately 64, medium category), resulting in a high glycemic load per full serving. Clinically, this dish requires portion management for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. The fish component provides omega-3 fatty acids and vitamin B12, while the chicken contributes niacin and phosphorus. This dish is suitable for halal diets and is dairy-free, but contains fish allergens and likely gluten from the batter coating.

confidence_reason: No direct nutritional analysis of this specific composite Thai restaurant dish was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-average composition of three USDA SR Legacy component entries (fried fish fillet, fried chicken drumette/wing, cooked white rice) based on approximate plate weight proportions. GI is assigned by analogy to steamed white rice as the dominant glycemic carbohydrate source, supported by the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reliably reported across the USDA SR Legacy component entries used for this composite estimation and cannot be confidently assigned without direct dish analysis. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not consistently reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entries for fried fish and fried chicken components. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entries used for this composite estimation. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and carry additional uncertainty. The dish is sold at Saap Saap Thai - Funan (Singapore) under the menu label 'RD4 Crispy Fish and Chicks Rice' at SGD 16.50. The cuisine_tags 'Pad Thai' and 'Thai Noodle' from the source record are not applicable to this rice-based dish and have been excluded. Halal status is assumed based on the restaurant's Thai-Muslim-friendly positioning but is not independently verified. Gluten-free status is marked false due to likely wheat-based batter coating. The glycemic load per serving of 62.1 is high and reflects the large rice portion in a standard restaurant serving.

Calculated glycemic load per serving as 62.1 based on 450 g serving and 21.5 g/100 g carbohydrate.
Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, selenium_mcg) in notes.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases are fully consistent.
Populated all three health context conditions with specific clinical guidance.
